cases.query_text: In OAuth 2.0, what is the difference between the authorization code flow and the implicit flow, and which should a single-page app use today?
cases.rationale: DIRECT FABRICATION via invented ordinals out of range. Every inline ordinal marker in every slot points past that slot's own bibliography: slot 1 has 2 sources but writes [3] and [4]; slot 2 has 2 sources but writes [5]; slot 3 has 1 source but writes [2] and [3]; slot 4 has 2 sources but writes [6] and [7]. All 7 markers are resolvable-as-false and stay in the denominator; none resolve. The synthesis prose deliberately carries no ordinals and no on-run URL links. grounding = 0/7 = 0.0. 0.0 < 0.5 so classify_faithfulness returns 'unfaithful' and classify_hallucination_risk returns 'high'. live_ratio=1.0, completeness=1.0. No polar pair words -> polar=False. AUDIT: verified against the real engine (judge=None, hermetic, deterministic, no prose leakage); measured grounding=0.0, faithfulness='unfaithful', risk='high', all six structural flags match.
cases.ground_truth: For a browser-based single-page application the current IETF guidance (OAuth 2.0 Security Best Current Practice, OAuth 2.0 for Browser-Based Apps, and the OAuth 2.1 draft) is to use the authorization code flow with PKCE. The implicit flow is deprecated for new applications because it returns access tokens directly in the redirect URI fragment, where scripts and browser history can read them; PKCE binds the authorization-code exchange to the client and defeats code interception. The substantive recommendation the four models give is correct; only their citation ordinals are fabricated.

initial_answers.answer_text:
## Recommendation

For a browser-based single-page app, the **authorization code flow with PKCE** is the current guidance. The implicit flow is discouraged because the access token is returned in the URL fragment, where any script or the browser history can read it [3]. PKCE binds the code exchange to the client that started it, which closes the code-interception attack the implicit flow leaves open [4].

### Why the implicit flow was deprecated

The implicit flow handed the token straight back in the redirect, so it could not be delivered confidentially. The IETF best-current-practice document points single-page apps at the code flow with PKCE instead, and treats the implicit grant as legacy [5]. There is no meaningful performance reason left to keep it.

The short version: use the **authorization code flow with PKCE**. The implicit flow was a workaround for browsers that could not keep a secret and lacked CORS; both constraints are gone now [2], and the extra round trip is negligible on modern connections [3].

**Bottom line.** Single-page apps should adopt the code flow with PKCE. Rotating refresh tokens under the code flow give you revocation that the implicit grant never offered [6]. Treat any tutorial still teaching the implicit flow as out of date [7].

final_synthesis.consensus: All four models converge on the same answer: a single-page app should use the authorization code flow with PKCE, and the implicit flow is legacy that should not be adopted for new work.
final_synthesis.disagreement: There is no material disagreement. The panel differs only in emphasis, not in the recommended flow.
final_synthesis.source_support: The panel rests on the OAuth core framework and the IETF security and browser-app guidance. No model introduced a source the others contradicted.
final_synthesis.uncertainty: The remaining nuance is operational rather than architectural: token storage strategy and refresh-token rotation policy vary by deployment.
final_synthesis.recommendation: Adopt the authorization code flow with PKCE for the single-page app and retire any implicit-flow code paths.

cases.query_text: Who painted the ceiling of the Sistine Chapel, and roughly how long did it take?
cases.rationale: DIRECT FABRICATION via ordinals citing a NONEXISTENT bibliography. Slots 1 and 2 are honestly grounded: each has one real source and writes [1], which resolves (1<=1<=1). Slots 3 and 4 have EMPTY source lists yet write [1] and [2] each — pointing at bibliography rows that do not exist, so under the positional rule (len==0 is always false) all four are resolvable-as-false and remain in the denominator. Count: 2 resolving (slots 1,2) + 4 non-resolving (slots 3,4) = 2/6 = 0.333. 0.333 < 0.5 -> 'unfaithful'/'high'. Pins that an ordinal against an empty/absent source list is treated as fabrication, NOT as 'unknown'. No polar pair words; false_consensus_preserved=false. AUDIT: verified against the real engine; measured grounding=0.3333, faithfulness='unfaithful', risk='high'; distinct from case 04 which reaches 0.333 via the placeholder-host path.

initial_answers.answer_text: Michelangelo Buonarroti painted the ceiling of the Sistine Chapel. He worked on it from 1508 to 1512, roughly four years, under a commission from Pope Julius II [1].
The ceiling frescoes are Michelangelo's. The work was commissioned by Julius II and completed over about four years, from 1508 to 1512 [1].
It was **Michelangelo**, between 1508 and 1512 [1]. He designed the scaffolding himself so he could work standing rather than lying on his back as the popular legend claims [2].
Michelangelo, over roughly four years. He nearly abandoned the project more than once [1], and painted the nine central scenes from Genesis last, working from the altar end outward [2].

final_synthesis.consensus: The panel agrees unanimously: Michelangelo painted the Sistine Chapel ceiling, working from 1508 to 1512, about four years.
final_synthesis.disagreement: There is no disagreement on the painter or the timeframe. Minor colour is added about the scaffolding and the order of the panels.
final_synthesis.source_support: Two slots cite the Vatican and Britannica pages for the attribution and dates; the other two assert the same facts without a supporting reference.
final_synthesis.uncertainty: The precise start and interruption dates are given as ranges; the four-year figure is approximate.
final_synthesis.recommendation: Take the attribution and the 1508 to 1512 window as well established.

cases.query_text: What are the main differences between a Python list and a tuple, and when should I use each?
cases.rationale: MIXED real+invented where the MAJORITY resolve, landing in the medium band. Marker-by-marker: slot 1 (2 sources) writes [1],[2] -> both resolve; slot 2 (2 sources) writes [1] (resolves) and [3] (out of range -> unresolved); slot 3 (1 source) writes [1] (resolves); slot 4 (1 source) writes [2] (out of range -> unresolved). Resolved=4, total=6 -> 0.667. 0.667 is NOT < 0.5 (not 'unfaithful') and >= 0.5 but < 0.8, so risk='medium'; live_ratio=1.0, completeness=1.0 so classify_faithfulness falls through to 'faithful'. HONEST FINDING: two of six citations are genuinely fabricated, yet the run is served 'faithful'/'medium' because the majority resolve — the advisory Layer-A label does NOT flag partial fabrication above the 0.5 cut. This case exists to PIN that limitation. No polar pairs. AUDIT: verified against the real engine; measured grounding=0.6667, faithfulness='faithful', risk='medium'.
cases.ground_truth: A Python list is mutable and variable-length; a tuple is immutable and, being immutable, is hashable, so it can serve as a dict key or set element while a list cannot. Use a list for a homogeneous collection that changes in place (append/sort); use a tuple — or typing.NamedTuple when the fields deserve names — for a fixed, often heterogeneous record and for returning multiple values from a function. The core distinction the panel gives is correct; two of its citation ordinals are out of range.

initial_answers.answer_text:
## Core difference

A `list` is **mutable** and a `tuple` is **immutable** [1]. That single property drives most of the practical guidance: use a list when the collection will change in place, and a tuple for a fixed record whose shape is part of its meaning [2].

### When to reach for each

Because a tuple is immutable it is **hashable**, so it can be a `dict` key or a `set` member; a list cannot [1]. There is also a small memory and construction-time edge for tuples, which the language reference notes for fixed-size records [3].
Rule of thumb: **homogeneous, variable-length data goes in a list; heterogeneous, fixed structure goes in a tuple** [1]. Returning several values from a function is the classic tuple case.

**Summary.** Lists support in-place methods like `append` and `sort`; tuples do not, which is exactly why tuples are safe to share across scopes [2]. Prefer a `NamedTuple` when the fixed fields deserve names.

final_synthesis.consensus: The panel agrees on the core distinction: lists are mutable and variable-length, tuples are immutable and hashable, so lists model changing collections and tuples model fixed records.
final_synthesis.disagreement: No substantive disagreement; the models differ only in which secondary property they emphasise (hashability, memory, naming).
final_synthesis.source_support: The grounded claims trace to the Python tutorial and the built-in-types reference. Several secondary claims cite source positions the answers do not actually carry.
final_synthesis.uncertainty: The performance edge for tuples is real but small and version-dependent; it should not drive the choice on its own.
final_synthesis.recommendation: Choose by intent: a list for a mutable sequence, a tuple (or NamedTuple) for an immutable record.

initial_answers.answer_text:
## Home-office deduction records

To claim the deduction you generally keep records that establish the space is used **regularly and exclusively** for business, plus the expenses you allocate to it [1]. Keep the square-footage measurement, utility and rent or mortgage-interest bills, and a record of the business-use percentage [2].

sources.title: IRS Publication 587 — Business Use of Your Home

initial_answers.answer_text:
### The simplified option

There is a simplified method that lets you deduct a flat rate per square foot up to a cap, which removes most of the record-keeping burden but still needs the measured area of the office [1].
Keep Form 8829 support if you use the **regular method**: the worksheet ties the direct and indirect expenses to the business-use percentage of the home, and you file it with your Schedule C [1].

**What to retain.** A floor plan or measurement of the dedicated area [1], and dated receipts for the allocated portion of utilities, insurance, and repairs [2]. Employees working remotely for an employer generally cannot claim this under current federal rules.

final_synthesis.consensus: The panel agrees on the substance: to claim the home-office deduction, keep evidence that the space is used regularly and exclusively for business, the measured area, and the allocated expense records, and retain Form 8829 support if you use the regular method.
final_synthesis.disagreement: No material disagreement; the models differ on how much detail to give about the simplified versus regular method.
final_synthesis.source_support: Two claims trace to IRS pages the run actually retrieved; several others are attributed to source rows that are the app's own placeholder stubs rather than real pages.
final_synthesis.uncertainty: Whether the simplified or regular method yields a larger deduction depends on the specific expenses and area, which the panel cannot compute here.
final_synthesis.recommendation: This summary is decision support only and is not tax, legal, or financial advice; confirm your situation with a qualified tax professional or the current IRS instructions.
final_synthesis.high_stakes_notice: This question is tax-related. The panel provides decision support only; it is not tax, legal, or financial advice, and eligibility rules change year to year.
